questions.question: Explain the difference between linear list and hash table directory implementation with their time complexities.
keyPoints: Linear list stores entries sequentially and requires O(n) search time
Hash table uses hash function providing O(1) average-case lookup
Linear list is simple to implement but slow for large directories
Hash table may have collision issues affecting performance

questions.question: Describe single-level and two-level directory structures with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Single-level: all files in one directory, simple but names must be unique
Two-level: MFD with user directories (UFD), allows same file names for different users
Single-level disadvantage: name conflicts, no organization
Two-level advantage: user isolation, simple maintenance

questions.question: What are the operations that can be performed on a directory? Explain any three.
keyPoints: Create: creates new directory entry
Delete: removes directory entry
Rename: changes file name
List: displays directory contents
Search: finds file by name
Open/Close: establishes access to file

questions.question: Explain the concept of absolute and relative path names with examples.
keyPoints: Absolute path starts from root directory (/home/user/file.txt)
Relative path is from current working directory (docs/file.txt)
Current directory represented by '.' and parent by '..'
Relative paths are shorter but depend on current directory

questions.question: What is an inode? What information does it contain?
keyPoints: Inode is index node storing file metadata
Contains: file size, creation/modification timestamps, permissions
Contains pointers to data blocks (direct, indirect)
Each file has unique inode number for identification

questions.question: Discuss directory caching and its benefits in file systems.
keyPoints: Directory caching stores frequently accessed directory entries in memory
Reduces disk I/O operations significantly
Improves file system performance for path resolution
Cache invalidation needed when directory structure changes

questions.question: Explain hierarchical directory structure and its advantages over single and two-level systems.
keyPoints: Hierarchical (tree-structured) allows unlimited nesting of subdirectories
Users can organize files logically into folders
Supports absolute and relative path naming
Overcomes limitations of single and two-level systems

questions.question: Describe in detail the various directory implementation techniques. Compare them based on time complexity, advantages, and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Linear list: simple implementation, O(n) search, efficient for small directories
Hash table: O(1) average lookup, collision handling needed, good for large directories
Linked list: efficient insertion/deletion, O(n) search, good for dynamic directories
FAT-based: linked list of clusters, used in early file systems
Inode-based: used in UNIX, stores metadata and block pointers
Comparison should include time/space complexity and use cases

questions.question: Explain the UNIX inode-based directory implementation. How does it handle file metadata and data storage?
keyPoints: Inode stores all file metadata: size, permissions, timestamps, link count
Contains array of direct block pointers (typically 12)
Contains single, double, triple indirect pointers for large files
Directory entries map file names to inode numbers
Block pointers form tree structure for efficient access to any file offset
Example calculations for maximum file size possible

questions.question: Discuss file sharing and protection mechanisms in directory implementation. How does the operating system ensure data integrity when multiple users access files?
keyPoints: File sharing: multiple users access same file through directory
Protection: access rights (read/write/execute) stored in inode
User identification (UID) and group identification (GID) for permissions
Synchronization mechanisms: file locking, semaphores for concurrent access
Critical section handling to prevent race conditions
Various locking strategies: advisory vs mandatory locking

questions.question: Design a directory structure for a university management system. Justify your design choices considering organization, access control, and efficiency.
keyPoints: Hierarchical structure: departments > batches > students/faculty
Separate directories for administration, academics, research
Access control lists for different user roles (admin, faculty, student)
Shared directories for common resources
Backup directories for archival
Efficiency considerations: caching, indexing
